messages.content: plato and laurence olivier had a close relationship?

As an AI language model, I do not have access to personal relationships or interactions between individuals. However, there is no evidence to suggest that Plato and Laurence Olivier had a close relationship as they lived in different time periods and belonged to different fields. Plato was a philosopher in ancient Greece, while Laurence Olivier was an actor and director in the 20th century.
good answer, thank you!
You're welcome! If you have any other questions, feel free to ask.
which word doesn't belong with the others? inch, kilogram, centimeter, yard
The word that doesn't belong with the others is kilogram. The rest of the words are units of measurement of length, while kilogram is a unit of measurement of mass.
there are 10 birds on a branch of a tree, if you shoot down one bird, how many are still there on the branch?
If you shoot down one bird from the branch of a tree, there would be 9 birds still left on the branch.
what is hullucinations

Hallucinations are sensory perceptions that are not based on any external stimuli or reality. They can affect any of the senses, such as seeing, hearing, feeling, smelling, or tasting things that are not real. Hallucinations can be caused by a variety of factors, including mental illnesses such as schizophrenia, physical conditions like neurological disorders, adverse reactions to medications or drugs, or sleep deprivation. Hallucinations can be very distressing and may affect a person's ability to function normally in their daily life. It is important to seek medical attention if experiencing recurrent or severe hallucinations.
what is Grounding/Knowledge Augmentation + Prompt Engineering in use commercial LLM

Grounding/Knowledge Augmentation and Prompt Engineering are two techniques that can be used in commercial Language Model (LM) systems like LLM (Large Language Model). 

Grounding/Knowledge Augmentation refers to the process of adding external knowledge to the LM system to improve its performance. This can include adding data from external sources such as knowledge graphs, dictionaries, encyclopedias, or other text sources to enhance the system's ability to understand and generate text.

Prompt Engineering, on the other hand, refers to the process of designing and optimizing prompts or input text to improve the quality and relevance of the LM's output. This can involve designing prompts that reflect specific domain expertise or using techniques such as controlled language, summarization, or simplification to improve the quality of the output.

Overall, these techniques can improve the performance of LLMs for commercial applications such as natural language processing, text generation, and conversational AI.
what's the difference between traditional NLP and gen ai based llm?

Traditional Natural Language Processing (NLP) is based on rule-based systems where predefined sets of rules and patterns are used to analyze and understand the language. Traditional NLP systems usually require significant amounts of manual engineering and maintenance to create and maintain language models, and their performance is limited to the set of rules and patterns they are programmed with.

In contrast, Generative Artificial Intelligence (Gen AI) based LLMs use machine learning techniques to develop their language models over time without human intervention. They utilize deep learning algorithms to analyze vast amounts of data, develop more generalizable patterns, and create models that are capable of generating more complex and diverse text outputs.

LLMs are large neural networks that can learn complex patterns in language and output very human-like text. They can generate text in response to a prompt, summarize information, or even compose text from scratch. 

So, the main difference between traditional NLP and Gen AI based LLM is that traditional NLP is generally rule-based and requires more human engineering and maintenance, while Gen AI based LLMs use deep learning techniques to generate language models that improve over time without human intervention.

make the following opening remarks better: :"After the very interesting “Talk of Terror” by Professor Ethan Mollick last week, we’re going to see two real-world applications of generative AI within Vanguard. We’re very happy to have our friends Balaji and Jithin from CAI are going to bring us the exciting demos of Infowave Crew Assist and Writer’s generative AI. After that, Terrence is going to share with us the overview of IMFS’ plan and strategy of generative AI for IMG. Hope you will enjoy today’s session. Now that, now let me switch to Balaji  "

Here is a better version of the opening remarks:

"Good afternoon, everyone. I hope you're doing well. Following on from last week's fascinating "Talk of Terror" by Professor Ethan Mollick, we look forward to exploring the practical applications of generative AI today. We are thrilled to have two talented individuals from CAI - Balaji and Jithin - who will showcase their exciting generative AI demos: Infowave Crew Assist and Writer's generative AI. After this, we invite Terrence to provide an overview of IMFS' strategy for generative AI in IMG. It promises to be an exciting session, and we hope you will find it engaging and informative. Without further ado, let's begin with Balaji."

make the following better: "The underpinning assumption of the RV signals is that all signals follow mean reversion. For back-testing, we want to see if the underpinning assumption holds in different regions. We want to evaluate if the spread is moving in the same direction or not as indicated by the RV signal.

Let’s assume we choose look-back window of 3 months and expect the mean-reversion happens in 30 days (holding period = 30 days). As shown in the graph below. If the current CUSIP is trading at px adj OAS of 100 bps, while it’s issuer curve (after curve fitting) at the same tenor shows 90 bps of px adj OAS, it means that the CUSIP is currently trading cheap relative to its issuer curve. If the signal of this CUSIP has a RV z-score of 1, which means that this CUSIP is currently trading at 1 stdev above the 3 months historical STC mean. If we hold this CUSIP for 30 days (expect the mean-reversion happens in 30 days), if the RV signal is the right indication, we are expecting it to be mean reverted, which means that the STC should becomes smaller regardless of the CUSIP or the IC moves. At T + 30, both scenarios shown in the graph on the right-hand side follow mean reversion, where we are expecting this CUSIP to become rich. Likewise, the opposite scenario exhibits a divergent dynamic."

Here is a suggestion for improving the given text:

"In our analysis of RV signals, we make the assumption that all signals follow mean reversion and seek to test this assumption across various regions for back-testing purposes. We aim to evaluate if the spread is moving in the same direction as indicated by the RV signal. 

Consider a look-back window of 3 months and an expected mean-reversion period of 30 days (i.e. holding period of 30 days). As depicted in the graph below, if a CUSIP is trading at a px adj OAS of 100 bps but its issuer curve (post-fitting) at the same tenor shows only 90 bps of px adj OAS, it implies that the CUSIP is trading cheap relative to its issuer curve. Now, if this CUSIP has a RV z-score of 1, meaning it is trading at 1 standard deviation above the 3-month historical STC mean, we hold this CUSIP for 30 days, and we expect the mean-reversion to happen, provided the RV signal is accurate. In this scenario, we expect the STC to become smaller, and the CUSIP should become rich. Both simulation scenarios shown in the graph on the right-hand side reflect mean reversion, regardless of the CUSIP or the IC moves. Conversely, if the CUSIP exhibits a divergent dynamic, the opposite scenario would hold true.

make the following better: "The underpinning assumption of the RV signals is that all signals follow mean reversion. For back-testing, we want to see if the underpinning assumption holds in different regions. We want to evaluate if the spread is moving in the same direction or not as indicated by the RV signal.

Let’s assume we choose look-back window of 3 months and expect the mean-reversion happens in 30 days (holding period = 30 days). As shown in the graph below. If the current CUSIP is trading at px adj OAS of 100 bps, while it’s issuer curve (after curve fitting) at the same tenor shows 90 bps of px adj OAS, it means that the CUSIP is currently trading cheap relative to its issuer curve. If the signal of this CUSIP has a RV z-score of 1, which means that this CUSIP is currently trading at 1 stdev above the 3 months historical STC mean. If we hold this CUSIP for 30 days (expect the mean-reversion happens in 30 days), if the RV signal is the right indication, we are expecting it to be mean reverted, which means that the STC should becomes smaller regardless of the CUSIP or the IC moves. At T + 30, both scenarios shown in the graph on the right-hand side follow mean reversion, where we are expecting this CUSIP to become rich. Likewise, the opposite scenario exhibits a divergent dynamic."

Below is a suggested rewording:

The RV signals are based on the assumption that all signals follow mean reversion. In order to perform back-testing, the validity of this assumption needs to be evaluated in different regions. Specifically, we need to examine whether spreads are moving in the same direction as the RV signal.

Suppose we choose a 3-month look-back window and expect mean reversion to occur in 30 days (holding period = 30 days). In the example below, if the current CUSIP trades at a px adj OAS of 100 bps, while its issuer curve (after curve fitting) shows 90 bps of px adj OAS at the same tenor, the CUSIP is considered to be trading cheap relative to the issuer curve. If the RV z-score of this CUSIP is 1, the CUSIP is trading at 1 standard deviation above the 3-month historical STC mean. We hold this CUSIP for 30 days and expect mean reversion to occur if the RV signal is the right indication. In such a scenario, we expect the STC to decrease regardless of the movements of the CUSIP or the IC. Both simulation scenarios shown on the right-hand side of the figure below follow mean reversion at T + 30, and we expect this CUSIP to become rich. Conversely, if the CUSIP exhibits a divergent dynamic, the opposite scenario would be expected.

make the following better: "The purpose of credit rover is to systematically search the IG market for relative value ideas and flag them to PMs/traders/analysts who can apply their judgement to filter further for actionable ideas. It is a tool designed to enable FIG to generate new sources of alpha within the funds, a one-stop shop for Relative Value (RV) - for PMs, Credit analysts and Traders, a centralized warehouse for all the data needed to make investment decisions, and a common place for Research and Trading to signal trade ideas and discuss them.

Credit Rover is part of a bigger platform called the Insights Platform. It aims to enable efficient market insights through intelligent data aggregation, interactive modules, and configurable alerts. Over time, the Insights platform should expand to support additional applications, especially given the volume of proprietary information we have—which grows every day."

Here is a suggestion for improving the given text:

"At its core, Credit Rover is a systematic tool built to identify relative value opportunities in the IG market and alert PMs, traders, and analysts to filter these ideas and generate new sources of alpha within the funds. It serves as a centralized warehouse for all the data required to make informed investment decisions, providing a one-stop-shop for Relative Value (RV), and a common place for research and trading to suggest and discuss trade ideas.

Credit Rover is part of a larger platform called the Insights Platform, which facilitates efficient market insights through intelligent data aggregation, interactive modules, and configurable alerts. As the platform continues to grow and evolve, it is increasingly able to support a range of additional applications, particularly given the vast and ever-expanding volume of proprietary information at our disposal."

make the following better: "RV stands for Relative Value. It is a quantitative analytical approach and is a systematic way to address the fundamental question: what’s expensive and what’s cheap? It allows us to identify relative misvaluations between and among securities, and if they improve the quality of the predictions we make about the future richening and cheapening of these securities. Credit RoVer uses mainly price adjusted OAS to generate relative value signals. The aim is to have one centralized place for all data RV everyone would agree upon.

Curve fitting is needed as the starting point for relative value. It allows us to judge which bonds are cheap or rich on an issuer's curve, as well as generate a long history of 5y, 10y, 30y, etc. points to make apples to apples comparisons across issuers for the purpose of relative value. In RoVer, what we effectively try to do is fit thousands of curves automatically and every day. Each issuer or benchmark has a specific curve. Not time efficient to build it out manually for each of them. Curve fitting can be used as an aid for data visualization (term structure), to infer values of a function where no data is available (new issues), and to summarize the relationship among two or more variables (maturity and model OAS)."

Here is a suggestion for improving the given text:

"Relative Value (RV) is a quantitative method that provides a systematic approach to answering the question: what's cheap and what's expensive? It enables us to identify relative discrepancies between securities. At Credit RoVer, we use price-adjusted OAS predominantly to generate RV signals. By collating all relevant data into one centralized location, RoVer aims to streamline the RV process and standardize its application.

Curve fitting is a fundamental component of RV. It enables us to differentiate between cheap and rich bonds on an issuer's curve, as well as comparing issuers on an apples to apples basis. To facilitate relative value analysis, RoVer has developed an automated process to fit thousands of curves every day across different issuers and benchmarks. This is a more efficient alternative to the manual process of building these curves. Curve fitting can also be used to aid data visualization, infer values of a function where data is lacking, and summarize relationships among variables, such as maturity and model OAS."

make the following better: "What we effectively do here is generate signals for the desk that help them narrow down potential opportunities to capture alpha. In RoVer, all signals have mean reversion as an underpinning assumption.

There are two types of signals, one is spread/standard RV, which focus on CUSIP vs curve signal; the other is regression RV, which illustrates curve vs curve signal. Both signals are generated based on price adjusted OAS.

Spread RV looks at distance of latest value of a specific point w.r.t. mean of that same point over a specific window. Each CUSIP has 10 different options of benchmark curves, either issuer curve, or 9 other benchmarks consisting of combination of sector and ratings. This method is typically used on the Bond Selection Page."

Here is a suggestion for improving the given text:

"Our goal is to generate signals that capture alpha opportunities for our desk. At RoVer, all signals are underpinned by the assumption of mean reversion.

There are two types of signals - spread/standard RV and regression RV - both of which are generated using price-adjusted OAS. The former compares CUSIPs to their benchmark curves, while the latter compares curves against each other.

Spread RV signals analyze the distance of the latest value of a specific point relative to the mean of that same point over a specific window. Each CUSIP has 10 different benchmark curve options: an issuer curve or nine other benchmarks made up of sector and rating combinations. This method is commonly used on the Bond Selection Page."

make the following better: "For example, the graph below looks at AAPL CUSIPs against AAPL issuer curve. The spread to curve (STC) is called the residual. We then take all STC/residuals from AAPL and find their mean. The RV signal/z-score is expressed is expressed in number of standard deviations away from mean. If the latest value is below mean, spread is considered expensive, and the RV signal is negative. If above mean, spread is considered cheap, and the RV signal is positive. Always with mean reversion as a base case."

Here is a suggestion for improving the given text:

"Consider the example graph below, which depicts AAPL CUSIPs relative to their corresponding AAPL issuer curve. The distance between the two lines is referred to as the Spread to Curve (STC), which is determined by calculating the residual. All STC residuals from AAPL CUSIPs are aggregated, and the mean is calculated. The RV signal (or z-score) is expressed in the number of standard deviations from the mean. If the latest value falls below the mean value, spreads are deemed expensive, and the RV signal is negative. If the latest value falls above the mean, spreads are deemed cheap, and the RV signal is positive. In all cases, mean reversion serves as the underlying principle for the RV analysis."
